I agree that including US _territories_ in the `state_abbr` is confusing.

The reason they're there is that the purpose of the address provider is to generate valid(ish) postal address, and the USPS uses the territories abbreviation as a postal state abbreviation.

I think we should add an argument to `state_abbr`. Something like `states_only=False`.

The reason I would default it to `False` is because `Faker` is often used for fuzzy testing, and this is a good example of an edge-case people don't usually think of.

I want to add my partial support to @astrofrog and @polymorphm on this issue. I repeated the github search, and find [close to one *million* matches](https://github.com/search?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=%22if+six.PY3%22+extension%3Apy&type=Code&ref=advsearch&l=&l=) for `if six.PY3`. And that's just one of several equivalent ways to write it. I would also like to link to [this related issue](https://github.com/requests/requests/issues/3914) for context.

I think it's unrealistic to expect enough of those people to fix their code in the roughly 3.5 years remaining until we may start seeing development releases of python 4.0, and I think both python 2.7 and six will still be alive and kicking when that time comes.

Look how everybody uses the `PY3` constant as if it was called `NOT_PY2`. I feel confident in saying that *none* of those who write it intends it to mean `if PY3 and not PY4`. I agree with astrofrog that it would be weird for it to behave as @polymorphm suggests when it's called `PY3`, but even so I think in this case practicality beats purity and the actual usage should guide this decision. The code search clearly shows that it would break expectations for `PY3` to be `False` on python 4. Therefore you really ought to change the definition to

    PY3 = sys.version_info[0] >= 3

... and update the documentation to match.

WIP of a SpyGlass lint implementation is available in https://github.com/imphil/fusesoc/tree/spyglass (Also featuring Jinja templating; I'll clean that up and split it out for #166).

Status:
1. It's a build tool, so it requires a separate *.core file with `backend = spyglass`. We need to find a solution here, in line with what was discussed above. 
2. It only writes the project file, doesn't perform the lint run yet
3. It doesn't allow to select a lint goal yet, I need to extend the `[spyglass]` section for that.

What's really blocking this now is the question how to integrate lint tools in general. Personally, I'd go with 
* Give up the distinction between simulation and build. Just make it an action. Each action roughly represents one tool run, e.g. "build with vivado", or "lint with verilator".
* If desired, the "actions" can be extended in two ways:
  1. Allow "superactions", like "build". This would (as it is today) figure out which build backend to use and then run the appropriate action. For example, if the user chooses the action "build", it looks up which tool to use from the core file (or optionally from the command line, as done for sim today), and then runs the "build-vivado" action
  2. Allow "subactions", like build-vivado.configure. This would just run one step of the action, in this case the writing of the project file for the vivado build backend.
* To avoid repeatedly removing and regenerating files (e.g. if the user runs build-vivado.configure first, and then build-vivado.build), the cached results should be used. Actually, that's the behavior of the pgm command already today.
* If the users wants multiple tools to be run as part of a build pipeline (e.g. first lint, then simulate, then synthesize for fpga, then flash) he/she easily can write a shell script or use whatever build pipeline tool he/she wants to use.

Hi, there,

It could be that there are two unrelated issues in your case: 1) the datatype of your X array and b the filler features. To tackle one problem at a time ... For the 2D scatterplot, you can only visualize 2 features at a time. So, if you have a  6-dimensional dataset, it will essentially be a 2D slice through this feature space with fixed values for the other 4 features.

For example, to plot the first two features with filler values for the other ones, you could do it as follows:

```python
value = 1.5
width = 0.75

fig, ax = plt.subplots()

plot_decision_regions(X, y, clf=my_classifier,
                      filler_feature_values={2: value, 3: value, 4: value, 5: value},
                      filler_feature_ranges={2: width, 3: width, 4: width, 5: width},
                      res=0.02, legend=2, ax=ax)
```

Let me know if that works for you

Hi, to set password in Active Directory you must use a secure TLS connection. If you're the Administrator of the Windows box you have to set up the certification authority and create a certificate for your AD instance. This is quite complex and depends on the Windows version you're using. You can anyway finds additional information in the Windows knowledge base.

Regarding the msNPAllowDialin attribute try to set it to the "TRUE" string:

```
conn.add('cn=alex4fun2,ou=ldap3-tutorial,dc=cc,dc=local', 'user', attributes={'msNPAllowDialin': 'TRUE'})
```

Note that in general, I think a rule of thumb is that in coreference or predicate conjunction or gapping, a variable is expanded where it is mentioned explicitly, and appears as a reentrancy where a pronoun is used or the argument is elided. So in the case of In the panel of six CRC cell lines , all of them harboured a KRAS gene mutation that was located in codon 12 or 13 . It makes sense to expand the variable `c3` when referring to the cell lines in the panel, and as a reentrancy when referred to as `them` as an argument of `harboured`. As another [example from the guidelines](https://github.com/amrisi/amr-guidelines/blob/master/amr.md#conjunctions), in The boy arrived and left on Tuesday. `boy` is expanded as an argument of `arrived` but used as reentrancy as an argument of `left` (where it is elided). I don't know how easy it is to take these issues into account, though.

Hi @BladeMight

First, this looks very convoluted to me. All the functions you used support key names instead of raw scan codes, and even combinations. So it could be `keyboard.press_and_release('shift+1')` instead of the whole bang thing. More importantly, there's already a function that writes text by simulating key presses: [`write`] (https://github.com/boppreh/keyboard/blob/master/README.md#keyboard.write). Hope that helps. 

About your issue, key press simulations are not shown in hooks, so you would have to physically press a key while `hkAction` is running. I believe the window of time is in the range of a few milliseconds, so it's expected you wouldn't catch it. The text may still be appearing in your application 9f choice, but the events were already submitted to the OS. There's no queueing in our side, but I presume the OS has a buffer for this. 

Does that answer your problem?

For what it's worth, I think it makes perfect sense to keep mouse & keyboard functionality together in the same library. System APIs usually tap both kinds of devices (at least for Windows). Why not Python modules?

Cheers,

I believe your Request is incorrectly coded.

From dumpasn1:
```
 93  29: . . . . SEQUENCE {
 95   9: . . . . . OBJECT IDENTIFIER ocspNonce (1 3 6 1 5 5 7 48 1 2)
106  16: . . . . . OCTET STRING 85 A4 46 39 3D BB 23 BD D0 A5 11 78 8F C5 EE 52
       : . . . . . }
```

The extension value is an OctetString with 16 bytes, your nonce.
According to the ASN.1 specs, however, the extension value shall be an OctetString, encapsulating another OctetString (i.e. your nonce). Details in rfc5280 (defines Extension(s)) and rfc6960 (defines the contents of your nonce extension).

I have reproduced the issue with pyodbc 4.0.16 under Python 2.7.13 on Windows. The Python statement ...

```python
cursor.execute("INSERT INTO #pyodbc_datetime_test VALUES (?, ?)", [pyodbc.version, now])
```

results in the following call (without fractional seconds) being logged in SQL Profiler:

```
exec sp_prepexec @p1 output,N'@P1 varchar(6),@P2 datetime2',N'INSERT INTO #pyodbc_datetime_test VALUES (@P1, @P2)','4.0.16','2017-05-06 04:10:25'
```

A workaround would be to cast the `now` variable from datetime to string ...

```python
cursor.execute("INSERT INTO #pyodbc_datetime_test VALUES (?, ?)", [pyodbc.version, str(now)])
```

... which results in a call like this (note that `@P2` is now varchar instead of datetime2) ...

```

Hi efialtisgr, I'm glad you are finding Pyrebase useful!

There are a couple of ways to approach this problem. 

The first is to set up an [index](https://firebase.google.com/docs/database/security/indexing-data#defining_data_indexes) in your security rules to allow for complex queries on specific properties in your database. In this example we'll be querying the ```match``` property, so our security rules might look something like this:
```
{
  "rules": {
    ".read": "true",
    ".write": "true",
    "xtrmp": {
      ".indexOn": ["match"]
    }
  }
}
```
Now we can run a query to check if a child with the ```match``` type of "FC BULLEEN LIONS vs. SOUTH MELBOURNE" exists, for example:
```
db = firebase.database()
matches = db.child("xtrmp").order_by_child("match").equal_to("FC BULLEEN LIONS vs. SOUTH MELBOURNE").get(user["idToken"])
if not matches.val():
    # data doesn't exist, we now know can push the data
```

That is one way to solve this. 

Another way to solve this problem is to save your data with custom made keys, rather than keys generated by firebase. Let's pretend we save all our matches with keys that follow the naming scheme of ```"TEAMvsTEAM"```, for example ```"BLvsSM"```:
```
db.child("xtrmp").child("BLvsSM").set(data, user["idToken"])
```
Now we'll be able to easily check if data exists in these locations, for example:
```
match = db.child("xtrmp").child("BLvsSM").get(user["idToken"])
if not match.val():
    # data doesn't exist, we now know can push the data
```

This is actually implemented and turned on by default for phrases of length
two. Run the "new hope" example and you'll see "death star" as a single
phrase, or "United States" for the Constitution.
This is controlled by the collocation parameter (which is the technical
term for the way phrases are detected).
This could be expanded to longer phrases, but I haven't done that, mostly
because I didn't need it and didn't have the time.
Pull request welcome. There's a reference in the code for the paper, or you
can check out nltk. I'm using a heuristic to discount the words that make
up the phrase, that would need to be extended to lager phrases, too, but
that shouldn't be a problem.

from __future__ import (absolute_import, division, print_function,
                        unicode_literals)

import argparse

import backtrader as bt
import backtrader.feeds as btfeeds
import backtrader.indicators as btind
from backtrader.utils import flushfile


class SMAStrategy(bt.Strategy):
    params = (
        ('period', 10),
    )

    def notify_order(self, order):
        print('-' * 50)
        print('order tradeid %d / price %.2f / size %d' %
              (order.tradeid, order.executed.price, order.executed.size))

    def notify_trade(self, trade):
        print('-' * 50)
        print('trade tradeid %d / price %.2f / size %d' %
              (trade.tradeid, trade.price, trade.size))

        self.trades[trade.tradeid] = trade

    def __init__(self):
        self.trades = dict()
        sma = btind.SMA(self.data, period=self.p.period)
        self.sig = btind.CrossOver(self.data.close, sma)
        self.tradeid = 0

    def next(self):
        if self.position.size < 2 and self.sig > 0:
            self.buy(tradeid=self.tradeid % 2)
            self.tradeid += 1

    def stop(self):
        for tradeid in sorted(self.trades):
            trade = self.trades[tradeid]
            print('-' * 50)
            print('tradeid %d / price %.2f / size %d' %
                  (trade.tradeid, trade.price, trade.size))


def runstrat():
    args = parse_args()

    # Create a cerebro entity
    cerebro = bt.Cerebro()

    # Add a strategy
    cerebro.addstrategy(SMAStrategy, period=args.period)

    # Load the Data
    data = btfeeds.BacktraderCSVData(dataname=args.dataname)

    # First add the original data - smaller timeframe
    cerebro.adddata(data)

    cerebro.broker.setcash(10000000)

    # Run over everything
    cerebro.run()

    # Plot the result
    cerebro.plot(style='bar')


def parse_args():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description='Trade Id Test')

    parser.add_argument('--dataname', required=False,
                        default='../../datas/2006-day-001.txt',
                        help='File Data to Load')

    parser.add_argument('--period', default=10, required=False, type=int,
                        help='Period to apply to indicator')

    return parser.parse_args()


if __name__ == '__main__':
    runstrat()
```

mistake

There are two ways of iterating over the components of an interval.

You can iterate on the connected components of an interval:

```python
>>> [x for x in interval([1, 2], 3)]
[(1.0, 2.0), (3.0, 3.0)]
```

Each component behaves like a named tuple:

```
>>> x = interval([1, 2], 3)
>>> x[0].inf
1.0
>>> x[1].sup
3.0
```

You can use the [components](http://pyinterval.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#interval.interval.components) attribute to iterate over the components so that each component is itself an interval:

```
>>> [x for x in interval([1, 2], 3).components]
[interval([1.0, 2.0]), interval([3.0])]
```

I updated the documentation to explain this difference.  I hope it is clear now.

In this case you could get this functionality with just 3 lines of application code. For example:

```python
import xlsxwriter

workbook = xlsxwriter.Workbook('test.xlsx')

worksheet1 = workbook.add_worksheet()
worksheet2 = workbook.add_worksheet('Foglio2')
worksheet3 = workbook.add_worksheet('Data')
worksheet4 = workbook.add_worksheet()

# Store the worksheet objects in a dict indexed by name.
my_worksheets = {}
for worksheet in workbook.worksheets():
    my_worksheets[worksheet.get_name()] = worksheet

# Retrieve a worksheet based on name:
worksheet = my_worksheets['Sheet1']
worksheet.write('A1', 'Hello')

worksheet = my_worksheets['Data']
worksheet.write('A1', 'Hello')

workbook.close()
```

RSS feeds don't really _have_ favicons per se, which is something of a shortcoming. As noted in the [https://jsonfeed.org/](JSON Feed) rationale:

> Also, to further reduce bandwidth use, publishers should provide the top-level `icon` and `favicon` URLs. When not present, feed readers will often attempt to find these things by 1) downloading the home page and scraping the HTML, and 2) making one or more requests to likely locations for these images. To reduce traffic on your server, put this info in the feed.

The open PR #109 adds support for JSON Feeds, including `icon`, but otherwise there is not favicon support in feedparser.

The thing that is interesting is that remote peers are accepted before the provider of the peers has been validated. I think we need to only accept remote peers from validated peers.  

If such a new peer fails validation, we should track it as stale or bad so we can recognize when the same ip returns. That will allow us to "ban" or progressively slow responses to known invalid peers.

It is possible to do what you described using `aioconsole.get_standard_streams`:

```python
async def main(prompt='>>> '):
    reader, writer = await get_standard_streams()

    def safe_print(msg):
        line = '\n' + msg + '\n' + prompt
        writer.write(line.encode() + current)

    asyncio.ensure_future(noise(safe_print))

    while True:
        current = b''
        writer.write(prompt.encode())
        while b'\n' not in current:
            current += await reader.read(1)
        line = current.decode().rstrip('\n')
        writer.write('You said: {}\n'.format(line).encode())
```

The problem is, you'll have to find a way to make `stdin` unbuffered (in order to get notified for every new character), and that's quite tricky to do with python3.

@afgane I've done something like this before, but I just looked for the limit on ```Running On-Demand %s instances' % instance_type``.

I agree that this would be nice, but there are a number of inherent problems...

1. If I were to change the names of the limits, existing code for many users would break.
2. The strings that I'm using to describe the limits are the same ones - or very close to those - used by Trusted Advisor, and by the APIs.
3. AWS's limits are really a lot less programmatic and structured than you'd originally think. There's no programmatic connection between a resource type and a limit. Some limits have names that make the connection clear, but there's no API or data element saying that "Running On-Demand c1.medium instances" is the limit of c1.medium's. In most services - certainly EC2 - from a customer perspective limits are an arbitrary key/value pair.

If you'd like a better example of this, take a peek at the Trusted Advisor Service Limits check, which returns some string limit names (which may or may not be the same as the limit name used by the service itself), along with a limit number and a usage number.

The people at AWS aren't even that clear on how all this works. There are a number of times I've opened support tickets asking for clarification on how a given limit is calculated, and had to wait a week to get a reply from someone on the service's development team, because nobody else knew.

__All that being said,__ there's a pretty simple solution to this... the EC2 instance limits all use formulaic names. Getting the dict that you want is as easy as (note I've truncated the long lines in this example):

```python
>>> c = AwsLimitChecker()
awslimitchecker 0.4.1@0.4.1 is AGPL-licensed free software; all users have a right to the full source code of this version. See <git@github.com:jantman/awslimitchecker.git>
>>> x = c.get_limits(service='EC2')
>>> x
{'EC2': {'Running On-Demand r3.xlarge instances': <awslimitchecker.limit.AwsLimit object at 0x7f86b79c3810>, 'Running On-Demand m3.xlarge instances': <awslimitchecker.limit.AwsLimit object at 0x7f86b79c34d0>, (truncated)
>>> res = {}
>>> import re
>>> for k, v in x['EC2'].items():
...     m = re.match(r'^Running On-Demand (\S+) instances', k)
...     if not m:
...         continue
...     res[m.group(1)] = v
... 
>>> res
{'m3.large': <awslimitchecker.limit.AwsLimit object at 0x7f86b79c3490>, 'd2.2xlarge': <awslimitchecker.limit.AwsLimit object at 0x7f86b79c3f10>, 'g2.8xlarge': <awslimitchecker.limit.AwsLimit object at 0x7f86b79ce050>, 'm3.2xlarge': <awslimitchecker.limit.AwsLimit object at 0x7f86b79c3510>, 'r3.4xlarge': <awslimitchecker.limit.AwsLimit object at 0x7f86b79c3890>, (truncated)
```

__Also__, I'm not sure what other python app you're going to be using this in, but please be aware of the licensing requirements around awslimitchecker.

Reversion doesn't have a concept of the "current' revision, it just has a revision history.

However, if you revert like this:

```python
with reversion.create_revision():
    version_list[2].revision.revert()
```

Then a new revision, representing the result of the revert, will be created at the top of the revision history. Your model will now match this.

The problem that you've got is that I've yet to expose a method to draw the next frame, so you need to reverse the logic and put your status into an Effect.  For example:

```
from asciimatics.effects import Print
from asciimatics.renderers import BarChart
from asciimatics.scene import Scene
from asciimatics.screen import Screen
from asciimatics.exceptions import ResizeScreenError, StopApplication
import sys
from random import randint

# Simple progress indicator - from 0 to 100
progress = 0


def get_progress():
    global progress
    if randint(0, 100) > 10:
        progress += 1
    return progress


class ProgressBar(Print):
    def __init__(self, screen):
        super(ProgressBar, self).__init__(
            screen,
            BarChart(
                7, 60, [get_progress],
                char=">",
                scale=100.0,
                labels=True,
                axes=BarChart.X_AXIS),
            x=(screen.width - 60) // 2,
            y=(screen.height - 7) // 2,
            transparent=False, speed=2)

    def _update(self, frame_no):
        global progress
        if progress >= 100:
            raise StopApplication("100% complete")
        else:
            super(ProgressBar, self)._update(frame_no)


def demo(screen):
    scenes = []
    effects = [
        ProgressBar(screen)
    ]
    scenes.append(Scene(effects, -1))
    screen.play(scenes, stop_on_resize=True)

while True:
    try:
        Screen.wrapper(demo)
        sys.exit(0)
    except ResizeScreenError:
        pass
```

Both GlobalSequenceAligner and StrictGlobalSequenceAligner uses Needleman-Wunsch. GlobalSequenceAligner is modified to not include the initial and terminal gaps in the result. This is a common practice in bioinformatics. It is sometimes called _semiglobal_ or _glocal_ alignment.

Elements are assumed to have the equivalence relationship: They represent the same thing if and only if they are equal. If you have non-trivial objects as elements, you can implement `__eq__` and `__hash__` methods in a way that satisfies the equivalence relationship.

I wrote this library out of necessity and one of my requirements was to compare all pairs of sequences in a set. That's why I needed the vocabulary: You can encode all sequences once, perform the alignments on fast integer arrays and decode only the alignments that you find useful. I think that's a very common use case for alignment tasks and I wouldn't want to lose the performance gain that comes with vocabularies. However, if you think you can generalize the existing algorithms without causing extra lag and without duplicating the code, you can go for it. I'm sure Python's duck typing can handle it :)

I'm not sure how many people are actively using the library but I'm aware that several people used it in the past. I would want to break their code (nor mine). Constant time backtracing can definitely be implemented as an extension to the existing code. If you need to get rid of the vocabulary though, I would like to see the end result before committing to it.

Hello,
Thanks for your time and interest. Well, this exception is normal and expected. I know for sure that projects depend upon it. It allows detecting race conditions, among other errors.
You should not normally save objects in a `post-save` handler anyway. If you need to change some fields, the right approach usually is overriding `save()` or installing a `pre-save` handler (which does not save at all: save is about to run anyway).

When a cached item is end of life, the current value will be cached again for `refresh_timeout` seconds. An async task is issued which will update the value and cache for `lifetime` seconds. If the refresh fails, it will be retried every `refresh_timeout` seconds until successful executed. I think this should solve your issue.

For example: lifetime = 300 (5m), refresh_timeout = 30 -> cache weather for 5m, after the lifetime remember the data for another 30 seconds and issue refresh task. If the task fails, cacheback will retry to refresh async after 30 seconds, and again, and again. If the async tasks finishes, the data will be cached for 5m again.

Regarding your issue, the problem is that we shouldn't save file extension in a file name, as you would end up with duplicated extension in Cloudinary url, like file.jpg.jpg. ```
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<root>
  <field_a>
    <list-item>foo</list-item>
  </field_a>
  <field_b>
    <list-item>foo</list-item>

</root>

{
    'field_a': {
        'list-item': 'foo'
    },
    'field_b': ['foo', 'bar']
}

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import zmq, zmq.asyncio, traceback, asyncio
from multiprocessing import Process, Event
import tempfile, os, pickle

BIG_FILE = '1' * 100000

NUM_PROCESSES = 1
NUM_CONCURRENCY = 70
MAX_CONCURRENCY = NUM_PROCESSES * NUM_CONCURRENCY

dummy_obj = {"1" * 1000: "1"*1000, "2": 2222, "3333": 4444}


_SEND_BUFFER_SIZE = 16 * 1024
_SEND_EMPTYMSG = True  # see https://github.com/zeromq/libzmq/issues/1922


class _EmptyMsg:
    def __init__(self):
        self.dummy = 'a' * _SEND_BUFFER_SIZE

class Terminator: pass

_empty_msg = _EmptyMsg()
_empty_msg_bytes = pickle.dumps(_empty_msg, pickle.HIGHEST_PROTOCOL)


# ZMQ employs fair-queueing for all receiving sockets.  This means even after we send all our messages, if another socket
# connects to the pull socket, a message that it sends will take priority over the pre-existing messages (cut to front of queue).
# To allow for Terminators to be received last, when a PULL socket sees a Terminator, it goes into poll mode and returns all
# items that are immediately available, and when no more are available it sends out the Terminator
# See discussion here: https://github.com/zeromq/pyzmq/issues/832
# Also note that you're only supposed to create one context per process: http://zguide.zeromq.org/page:all#Getting-the-Context-Right
class SocketCtx:
    def __init__(self, socket_type, high_water_mark: int, socket_name: str, connect_method_name: str, zmq_ctx: zmq.asyncio.Context, send_empty_msg=True, rcvtimeout=None):
        """
        Creates a ZMQ socket context object which ensures that `Terminator` is received last.

        :param socket_type: type of socket
        :param high_water_mark: high water mark
        :param socket_name: name to pass to connect_method
        :param connect_method_name: either "bind" or "connect"
        :param zmq_ctx: zmq.asyncio.Context to use (will not destroy during aexit).  Pass None to have this class construct and own the context.
        :param send_empty_msg: set to True if you would like to flush the socket during `aexit` with _EmptyMsg
        """
        self._zmq_ctx = zmq_ctx
        self._socket = None
        self._socket_type = socket_type
        self._socket_name = socket_name
        self._high_water_mark = high_water_mark
        self._connect_method_name = connect_method_name
        self._pending_terminator = None
        self._send_empty_msg = send_empty_msg
        self._rcvtimeout = rcvtimeout

    @property
    def name(self):
        return self._socket_name

    async def __aenter__(self):
        assert not self._socket

        self._socket = self._zmq_ctx.socket(self._socket_type)
        assert self._socket

        self._socket.RCVHWM = self._socket.SNDHWM = self._high_water_mark

        if self._rcvtimeout is not None:
            self._socket.RCVTIMEO = self._rcvtimeout

        if _SEND_EMPTYMSG:
            self._socket.SNDBUF = _SEND_BUFFER_SIZE

        getattr(self._socket, self._connect_method_name)(self._socket_name)

        return self

    async def __aexit__(self, exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb):
        try:
            if self._socket:
                if self._socket_type == zmq.PULL:
                    # pull everything from our in_queue so we don't hang on socket.close()
                    try:
                        while True:
                            await self.recv_pyobj_noblock()
                    except zmq.error.Again:
                        pass
                elif self._socket_type == zmq.PUSH and _SEND_EMPTYMSG and self._send_empty_msg:
                    for i in range(self._high_water_mark + 1):
                        await self.send(_empty_msg_bytes)
        finally:
            if self._socket:
                self._socket.close()
                self._socket = None

    async def send_terminator(self, **attributes):
        terminator = Terminator()
        for n, v in attributes.items():
            setattr(terminator, n, v)

        return await self.send_pyobj(terminator)

    async def recv(self, *args, **kwargs):
        return await self._socket.recv(*args, **kwargs)

    async def _recv_pyobj(self, *args, **kwargs):
        # this method will filter out _EmptyMsgs
        while True:
            pyobj = await self._socket.recv_pyobj(*args, **kwargs)
            if pyobj.__class__ is _EmptyMsg:
                continue
            return pyobj

    async def recv_pyobj_noblock(self):
        # If self._pending_terminator is set, will return all pending messages in the socket's in-queue and
        # then return the terminator last.  Otherwise will raise zmq.error.Again if there are no more pending messages.
        try:
            return await self._recv_pyobj(zmq.NOBLOCK)
        except zmq.error.Again:
            if self._pending_terminator:
                pyobj = self._pending_terminator
                self._pending_terminator = None
                return pyobj
            raise

    async def recv_pyobj(self, *args, **kwargs):
        if self._pending_terminator:
            return await self.recv_pyobj_noblock()

        pyobj = await self._recv_pyobj(*args, **kwargs)

        # If we got a Terminator, we store this off and first try to send any messages
        # that are available in the socket queue before returning the Terminator.
        if pyobj.__class__ is Terminator:
            self._pending_terminator = pyobj
            return await self.recv_pyobj_noblock()

        return pyobj

    async def send(self, data):
        return await self._socket.send(data)

    async def send_pyobj(self, obj):
        return await self._socket.send_pyobj(obj, protocol=pickle.HIGHEST_PROTOCOL)

async def async_proc_main(worker_rdy, in_queue_name, out_queue_name):
    zmq_ctx = zmq.asyncio.Context()

    async with SocketCtx(zmq.PULL, NUM_CONCURRENCY, in_queue_name, 'connect', zmq_ctx=zmq_ctx) as in_queue, \
            SocketCtx(zmq.PUSH, NUM_CONCURRENCY, out_queue_name, 'connect', zmq_ctx=zmq_ctx) as out_queue:
        worker_rdy.set()

        async def receiver():
            while True:
                try:
                    obj = await in_queue.recv_pyobj()
                    await out_queue.send_pyobj(obj)
                except:
                    traceback.print_exc()

        tasks = [asyncio.ensure_future(receiver()) for _ in range(MAX_CONCURRENCY)]
        await asyncio.gather(*tasks)


def proc_main(*args, **kwargs):
    loop = zmq.asyncio.ZMQEventLoop()
    asyncio.set_event_loop(loop)
    loop.run_until_complete(async_proc_main(*args, **kwargs))


async def async_main():
    zmq_ctx = zmq.asyncio.Context()
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()

    with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as temp_dir:
        in_queue_name = 'ipc://{}'.format(os.path.join(temp_dir, 'in_queue'))
        out_queue_name = 'ipc://{}'.format(os.path.join(temp_dir, 'out_queue'))

        workers = []
        rdy_events = []
        for _ in range(NUM_PROCESSES):
            rdy_evt = Event()
            p = Process(target=proc_main, args=[rdy_evt, out_queue_name, in_queue_name])
            p.start()

            rdy_events.append(rdy_evt)
            workers.append(p)

        for rdy_evt in rdy_events:
            await loop.run_in_executor(None, rdy_evt.wait)

        async with SocketCtx(zmq.PULL, MAX_CONCURRENCY, in_queue_name, 'bind', zmq_ctx=zmq_ctx, rcvtimeout=3000) as in_queue, \
                SocketCtx(zmq.PUSH, MAX_CONCURRENCY, out_queue_name, 'bind', zmq_ctx, send_empty_msg=False) as out_queue:

            async def sender():
                num_sent = 0
                while True:
                    try:
                        await out_queue.send_pyobj(dummy_obj)
                        num_sent += 1

                        if num_sent % MAX_CONCURRENCY == 0:
                            print("s", end='', flush=True)
                    except:
                        traceback.print_exc()  # this should never happen

            for _ in range(MAX_CONCURRENCY):
                asyncio.ensure_future(sender())

            num_rcvd = 0
            while True:
                try:
                    await in_queue.recv_pyobj()
                    num_rcvd += 1

                    if num_rcvd % MAX_CONCURRENCY == 0:
                        print("r", end='', flush=True)
                except zmq.error.Again:
                    pass
                except:
                    traceback.print_exc()  # this should never happen
                    raise

def main():
    loop = zmq.asyncio.ZMQEventLoop()
    asyncio.set_event_loop(loop)
    loop.run_until_complete(async_main())

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

On Tue, Oct 13, 2015 at 3:27 AM, Min RK <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> But the reason we did the repo split was the enable
> higher level components (ipywidgets) to move quickly and break things.
>
> Yes, I don't mean that this applies to everything, but traitlets is
> probably where we should be the *most* conservative about breakage. Part
> of the reasoning of the split is that we have components at widely varying
> levels of stability—some very stable, some very unstable. Widgets are
> *highly* unstable and experimental, and I view as free to break all the
> things, while traitlets *was* quite stable. Most of the other packages
> lie somewhere in-between.
>
> Traitlets is challenging because it is at the very bottom, but also used
> extensively by ipywidgets in advanced ways that require its evolution (not
> breakage necessarily though).
>
> Yup, traitlets is a tricky one because of that. I'm AOK with new APIs, and
> as the work here has demonstrated so far, we can make new APIs without
> unnecessarily breaking the old ones. They can be deprecated and stay
> deprecated for quite some time. Deprecating doesn't need to put a ticking
> clock on removal, either—we don't have to remove deprecated APIs until
> maintaining them becomes a problem.
>
> Even though we don't want to be forced to do lock-step releases of all
> packages, that doesn't mean we *can't*, when needed.
>
> Quite true, but hopefully it will never (or rarely) be needed. I doubt we
> will ever be in a position to need a release of traitlets that would break
> the stable version of any other project, for instance. I would expect
> sensible deprecation cycles to allow releases of other packages to have
> been made naturally in the meantime.
>
> I think I am mostly reacting possibly over-generalized idea of "no API
> breakage for 2 years". We still have a lot of things to break in parts of
> our code base...
>
> I didn't mean to imply that I was trying to dictate a strict rule,
> especially one that would apply to every one of our repos the same, sorry
> about that. I was just trying to convey the idea that we should be
> conservative about breaking widely used low-level APIs on our most stable,
> core packages. In general, I think we should do our best to do more of
> exactly what's been done here: make new APIs adjacent to old ones and
> deprecate more gently, with the deprecation time (time matters much more
> than version numbers for deprecations) taking into account how much code
> needs to be updated to use the new APIs. I think at least a year for
> _foo_changed to keep working is a reasonable ballpark estimate for
> something like that. I didn't mean for the statement to be stronger than
> that, though.

that's by design; for boto calls that list entities, it caps the max returned items to a certain number (for `IAM.list_users()` it's 100).  if you have more than 100, you will have to _paginate_.  for the initial call, the returned entity will have a `Marker` field...you will use that as parameter for the next call to get the next 100, then use the `Marker` field for that call for the next, etc.  

More information [here](http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/iam.html?highlight=IAM#IAM.Client.list_users)

`git_filter` is for creating your own in-code filters to be called by libgit2. They would not affect git or its idea of changes. Filter for git are things like crlf or the clean and smudge filters you can have for stuff like compressing files or encrypting them which libgit2 would not take into account. There's also textconv but IIRC that does not change the idea of change, just the textual output.

What *are* the differences in that file that doesn't get recognised? There are times when some versions of git will show an empty diff for a particular file due to implementation details, which libgit2 would not do.

>>> from fuzzywuzzy.fuzz import ratio

The following proof-of-concept works for retrieving the JWT token:

````
import woocommerce.api

wcapi = woocommerce.api(
    url="https://DOMAIN",
    consumer_key=WOO_CONSUMER_KEY,
    consumer_secret=WOO_CONSUMER_SECRET,
    wp_api=True,
    version="jwt-auth/v1",
)

wcapi.post("token", {"username": "test", "password": "test"})
# this adds consumer details as oauth 1.0 credentials to URL, but they don't seem to interfere
# tested oauth-in-url method with postman and same result there
````

returns

````
<Response [200]>
{'token': 'ALONGTOKEN',
 'user_display_name': 'Display Name',
 'user_email': 'email@example.com',
 'user_nicename': 'nicename'}
````

...the token value now needs to be included in the `Bearer` header of subsequent queries (rather than the OAuth 1.0 consumer key + consumer secret).

This is an expected behavior of MongoDB. If you don't explicitly set `limit`, txmongo will send `None` for `n_to_return` and MongoDB will return up to 101 docs in the first batch and up to 16Mb in each following. This is documented here: https://docs.mongodb.com/manual/tutorial/iterate-a-cursor/#cursor-batches (in Tutorials somewhy).

We are using `n_to_return` only to specify maximum number of documents we want to get and only if `limit` is specified. This is not the same as `batchSize`.

This's how I use it currently in our production code


```python
from django.db import models
from rest_framework import serializers

from enumfiels import IntEnum
from enumfiels import EnumIntegerField
from enumfields.drf.serializers import EnumSupportSerializerMixin

class Order(models.Model):
    class State(IntEnum):
        PENDING, PRINTING, SHIPPING, ERROR, CANCELED, DELIVERED = xrange(6)

        class Labels:
            PENDING = 'Pending'
            PRINTING = 'Printing'
            SHIPPING = 'Shipping'
            ERROR = 'Error'
            CANCELED = 'Canceled'
            DELIVERED = 'Delivered'

    state = EnumIntegerField(State, default=State.PENDING)

class OrderSerializer(EnumSupportSerializerMixin, serializers.ModelSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = Order
        fields = '__all__'
```

The DRF View will now accepts/returns `state` with as integers (with values in the `State` definition) since we're using `IntEnum`.

Thanks @amitripshtos. In general I would not advise using the synchronous producer if speed is a major concern for you. The producer in synchronous mode waits for a delivery report for every single message it sends, which under librdkafka can take a long time. It's possible that there are some optimizations we can make to the rdkafka-based synchronous producer, but it's not a particularly high priority since synchronous mode has never been about speed.

If the reason you're interested in using the synchronous producer is to have stronger guarantees about message delivery, you might consider using the asynchronous producer's delivery reports feature. A common pattern for this is copied here from the README:
```python
>>> with topic.get_producer(delivery_reports=True) as producer:
...     count = 0
...     while True:
...         count += 1
...         producer.produce('test msg', partition_key='{}'.format(count))
...         if count % 10 ** 5 == 0:  # adjust this or bring lots of RAM ;)
...             while True:
...                 try:
...                     msg, exc = producer.get_delivery_report(block=False)
...                     if exc is not None:
...                         print 'Failed to deliver msg {}: {}'.format(
...                             msg.partition_key, repr(exc))
...                     else:
...                         print 'Successfully delivered msg {}'.format(
...                         msg.partition_key)
...                 except Queue.Empty:
...                     break
```

@yungchin I think it should provide a parent exception which means a uncoverable failure. So that the user of pykafka can determine if he should stop the consumer and start a new one.

BTW, I have found three problems:
1. it should remove all watches and all ephemeral nodes from zookeeper if the zookeeper client is from outside and the consumer is not running.
2. if the autostart flag is set, it should catch all exceptions from `start()`, then stop the consumer. Otherwise, those started threads will run forever.
3. like #223, it should be a broker connection failover problems. It hangs when the producer is producing messages, and [retry logic of producer when socket disconnect exception is catched](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/master/pykafka/producer.py#L168-L177) never makes sense.

I agree that working with low precision units is a bad idea as it introduces artifacts. That is something you learn eventually when doing data processing and data acquisition. To address this problem, matlab and numpy both default to foat64 which is likely overkill in many situations, but definitely safe for non-experts.

That said, having the ability to develop with high precision units, and speed up computation with low precision ones is invaluable once a processing pipeline is ready to be optimized. The example you gave is great if people are trying to differentiate between data that is close to the center of the distribution, but if the data is well separated, it might not matter and the "error" might be acceptable. The user should have that choice.

My 2 cents on data conversion:
  * If an algorithm only supports one input type, for example, `rank` algorithms don't support floats, or 32bit datatypes, then it should be up to the user to explicitly convert his data to the appropriate type and in the correct range. 16 bit datatypes are also ambiguous as to whether the upper 12 or lower 12 bits should be used.
  * Forcing an image to be between `0.` and `1.` is a really narrow view of what image data is. What if we are analyzing airflows? or temperature data? moisture level in the soil? topographic data? Do we force the users to work with normalized units? The calling `img_as_*` as opposed to throwing an error is just so domain specific in my mind. This is why I spent some time trying to make warping work with complex numbers.

Well, using poor inline assembly codes (like what I've wrote) is admittedly fragile. It surely causes tricky issues. (E.g. https://stackoverflow.com/questions/12221646/how-to-call-cpuid-instruction-in-a-mac-framework).

Using the `target` attribute seems to be reasonable. Its support is actually not so recent-ish (from 4.8: https://gcc.gnu.org/wiki/FunctionMultiVersioning), and waiting Clang's support is acceptable. Even ARM NEON support would be possible in future :) Intel C Compiler also has a similar feature, so someone interested can write a patch to support it.

Regarding manual detection, the problem is there is no open-source simple, portable, easy-to-use and stable library for CPU detection. (https://wiki.linaro.org/LEG/Engineering/OPTIM/Assembly#Hardware_identification) Using `__builtin_cpu_supports` intrinsics is one possibility. It's support is in GCC 4.8.0 and Clang 3.9.0 or later. (Clang 3.7 and 3.8 had broken implementation: https://bugs.llvm.org/show_bug.cgi?id=25510). Numpy already uses this (https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/e0f884692f629d931d3673adc8dd6b0938cefa52/numpy/core/src/umath/cpuid.c). Intel C Compiler again has a similar feature and MSVC has a more primitive `cpuid` intrinsics, so supporting them is possible if desired.

In Hungary people often put family name before first name, too. And there are other issues: e.g. for CJK names, we may also want to store the original version in say Kanji, plus preferred transliteration in ASCII, if we want to cover various usage scenarios.

In the current MySQL based codebase, you could create optional `more_info` column, containing serialised key-value property store, like we do in bibdocs.  IOW, any wanted additional information about a user would be stored there as a Python dictionary. The buildout should extend these:

```
extends =
    http://download.zope.org/zopetoolkit/index/1.0.8/zopeapp-versions.cfg
    http://download.zope.org/Zope2/index/2.13.22/versions.cfg
# or newer http://dist.plone.org/versions/zope-2-13-24-versions.cfg
# but this gives a constraint error for mechanize.
```

And the `zopepy` part can simply be this:

```
[zopepy]
recipe = zc.recipe.egg
eggs =
    Products.CMFCore
# or Zope2 as before, but using CMFCore seems saner.
interpreter = zopepy
scripts = zopepy
```

The current function outputs how many objects are in the catalog total and how many passed the initial cuts, since I thought those were interesting bits of information people might want.  But you could certainly add additional lines there that you think would be useful.

The other option would be to override `getKwargs`. The biggest reason I like Terminator's split panes better than Tmux's is that when I have split the window into two panes side by side, I easily select multiple lines of text in one pane with the mouse. If I try to do the same thing with Tmux, it will instead select the lines from both panes, including the pane separator.

Additionally, when text in one pane is scrolling very fast, I cannot select text in the other pane. This is all because Tmux handles its own panes, whereas Terminator uses the window manager's widgets and hence behaves natively.

I guess these are not problems for people who don't use the mouse, but I find the mouse very useful with a terminal emulator like Guake when the terminal covers half the screen and is always on top, so I can select text from a web browser and middle-click-paste it to the relevant terminal pane very quickly.

Everybody agrees to drop `format` and `type` metadata ?

Well there's two ways of looking at it. Scott is right that you only need 180 degrees for elevation in a polar coordinate system.  Only allowing 180 degrees though means you need to handle modifying elevation as a function of azimuth which can make a few things confusing (Cross zenith and you need to rotate the azimuth to the new direction).  This would also break a lot of the fixed angle variable (You now need to break up horizon-horizon into two sweeps).

While less mathematically correct, [-180,180] avoids these issues (As does 0-360). 

We (and many others) do a lot of below the horizon scanning, though we often don't release all of those datasets (A lot are for trouble shooting and maintenance).

We can always just mandate(suggest) azimuth and leave elevation to the individual formats as well.

And actually, in reference to Issue 1 above, there is still a problem with the `auth` kwarg. If I pass it to customize my authentication for token refresh  and the tokens is NOT expired, then the kwarg is not popped, and is passed to `request()`. On the other hand, if the token is expired, the kwarg is popped and NOT passed to `request()`.

The API around this is really complicated, since refresh_token() args can come from kwargs to `refresh()` or `auto_refresh_kwargs`. OR auth is auto-generated in certain cases.

To simplify this would be a breaking change, it would require relying solely on auto_refresh_kwargs, so the caller has explicit control over the authentication. I think it is probably what is wanted since providers are free to implement their own methods...

Thoughts?

```python
self.connection.transaction(None)
```
doesn't get a chance to occur either in `Transaction.commit()` or `Transaction.rollback()`.

Once we rely on `Batch.__exit__` to just put the current `Transaction` off the stack, this bug should go away.

@DingkunLiu 可以说所有分类算法都依赖特征提取，随机森林只是准确率高点罢了。

特征工程是数据集相关的，所以一般是手动。并且通常在数据挖掘流程中占80%的工作量。

所以你如果打算绕过，就得了解它的特征选取了啥，然后在特征空间中让正向分类和负向分类重叠在一起。用通俗的话来说就是混淆。

比如 AES 的密文的字符分布是有规律的，那就插入点别的字符破坏这种规律。
我用vultr洛杉矶节点，使用SS目前比较稳定，使用加密：aes-128-cfb，无混洗，还是比较担心某天挂了，然后用另一家VPS厂商搭建SS+obfs，还好两个目前都无掉线情况，不然就悲剧了！

个人感觉，未经验证：HTTP的特征估计不是在header或者body。HTTP本身半双工的特性，用于承载多个并发的payload的话感觉应该表现得和普通HTTP请求非常不一样。如果有征对性的检查行为特征而不是内容或者混淆算法，也应该能比较容易的分辨出来。
其实很多时候实际上使用的侦测方法远比我们想像的简单。例如南方某些城市的KCPTun出问题其实是UDP大包流量持续超高就封端口，虽然有误杀，但是很有效。。。
这个思路也是还可以继续发展下去的，或许HTTP2或者websocket可以搞一搞。

The API doesn't support this, you'll need to dig into the XML with lxml and some maybe some python-docx internals support if you want it bad enough :)

The general gist of the XML is here: 
http://python-docx.readthedocs.org/en/latest/dev/analysis/features/shapes-inline.html#specimen-xml
and here:
http://python-docx.readthedocs.org/en/latest/dev/analysis/features/picture.html#specimen-xml

You can get a handle to the wp:inline element using InlineShape._inline:
https://github.com/python-openxml/python-docx/blob/master/docx/shape.py#L57

From there you can navigate to the pic:pic element using something like this:

    _inline.graphic.graphicData.pic.blipFill.blip

From there you'll need to parse the embed link to get the relationship to the picture, which will be stored as a separate part.

These are just general guidelines, I don't have time unfortunately to get it down to working details, but should give you an idea of what's involved. > ...it should be just a link or a button, not both.

Yes!!! :+1: 

And decision about one or the other should be based on the actual function/goal that the element is supposed to perform. Some good reads about `Links vs Buttons` from an accessibility point of view:

## [Links are not buttons. Neither are DIVs and SPANs](http://www.karlgroves.com/2013/05/14/links-are-not-buttons-neither-are-divs-and-spans/)

### TL;DR

> DIVs and SPANs are not buttons
> If it navigates, it is a link. Use link markup with a valid hypertext reference
> If it triggers an action, it is a button. Use a BUTTON element
> If you don’t like how a button looks, style it with CSS.

## [Links, Buttons, Submits, and Divs, Oh Hell](http://adrianroselli.com/2016/01/links-buttons-submits-and-divs-oh-hell.html)

### TL;DR
https://twitter.com/aardrian/status/734792836667805696

## [Links vs. Buttons in Modern Web Applications](https://marcysutton.com/links-vs-buttons-in-modern-web-applications/)

### Why is it important?
> If a screen reader user calls tech support and gets instructions to “click the button” in your UI that’s really coded as a link, they may have trouble finding it. Also, consider voice interfaces: if you say a command to click a button but it’s really coded as a link, you might have problems, no?

Sorry, I have to comment on this so late... I don't recall being referred to this PR when it was being triaged.

The code is difficult to read and it adds a very complex layer between the Makefile and the vanilla repository.

From my POV (as a package maintainer/Kolibri dev), I would like to be able to understand what happens to the repository when it's being built just by reading the Makefile.

But now I have to read this Python code full of indents and with very few comments and a very long docstring in the `__init__`. I've seen worse, but still :) My worry is not so much just the immediate addition, but:

1. What it will turn into over time
1. The aggregated amount of time trying to understand and debug this added complexity

Maybe I wasn't paying enough attention when this happened, so this high-level comment falls unfortunately late: I don't think that most of this logic even belongs in the source repository. Of course it should offer a level of customization, but the logic presented here makes it very blurry:

If a build customizes requirements, plugin sets and default configurations through a *build tool* maintained in Kolibri, then we have to basically maintain this within the release cycle and accommodate to lots of parametric stuff that could have been handled conventionally.

Ideally, the Kolibri source is isolated from all this nitty-gritty distribution logic. It will happily understand specific parameters, but this is not done in the build pipeline. That pipeline can be a crazy jungle of logic sitting in some other repo.

A mental model would be the Kolibri source sitting inside a very isolated shell that can only be penetrated by API :)

```
Installer logic options which the installer can just go nuts with:

 - its own requirements.txt with whatever it wants
   - additional plugins
   - theming
 - set default user account + system services
 - pre-seeding .kolibri/ dir
   - default plugin set
   - channels
 - set system environment for Kolibri
   - overwrite kolibri command
 - UI
 - some script that builds it all

 --------------------
 |  KOLIBRI SOURCE  |
 --------------------


                                  ..-^~~~^-..
                                .~           ~.
                               (;:           :;)
                                (:           :)
                                  ':._   _.:'
                                      | |
                                    (=====)
                                      | |
-O-                                   | |
  \                                   | |
  /\                               ((/   \))

(build)

```


The big question I would frame is:


## What is it that the Kolibri source does not accommodate for that could be specified by an installer using the source code?

Please open issues reflecting this, and we can fix it and remove this layer of build customization and instead create installers that start Kolibri with a pre-configured environment that they control themselves.

I'm aware that untrusted Javascript + untrusted server + trusted browser is the main threat model that the masking is designed to mitigate, but it wasn't obvious to me from the RFC whether that's the *only* situation where it's important.

For example, this architecture would obviously be insecure with autobahn's use of MT:
```
Untrusted javascript
         |
Trusted browser --- [Autobahn-based Websocket proxy] --- dodgy middleware --- untrusted server
```
...but that's a pretty weird topology, not sure why anyone would implement that.

Or there could be attacks involving a malicious server tricking an otherwise well-behaved client into sending some data that's under the server's control? I guess the simple version of this is: you should never run an autobahn-based echo client against an untrusted server, because that might allow that server to hijack unrelated browser sessions. Though again, I'm not sure why anyone would be running an echo client against random servers. But maybe it's more plausible that some websocket-based protocol would involve the client at some point sending data that originated on the server...

(I'm similarly uncertain on whether secure entropy is needed for the initial handshake nonce -- AFAICT it isn't? But autobahn actually does use os.urandom for this :-).)

@jaswilli I think there's an element of this which is just "how seamless can we make this?"
That's presumably the main drive for us being asked to solve this conflict.

I would rather tell people to use a virtualenv than tell them to use homebrew -- though in an ideal world the OSX users would be doing both.
It would be great if we could ask everyone to use homebrew, but I doubt we can get away with it.


The trouble with the CLI use-case is that part of its target audience is totally non-technical.
Excepting a dependency chain like this, there should never be someone installing the SDK who isn't comfortable with a virtualenv -- if there is, that person will have much bigger problems than just getting it up and running.

I spent some time last night looking into build/install software that the CLI could use.
That's a dark, dark place, and it frightens me, but there is [pyinstaller](https://github.com/pyinstaller/pyinstaller)
If we do a binary distribution for the CLI, I think we'd do OSX-only to reduce our testing burden.

I don't like not getting to use this library just because some people might be installing our package in a very bad way, but it's worth noting that a large number of internal people outside of the dev team ran into this when first doing installs of the CLI.
Given the choice between doing a lot more work to support the CLI, or just not using `six`, I think the choice is clear.
Our scariest dependency on `six` that I see is the `six.reraise` we're discussing in globus/globus-cli#26


I don't like any of the paths forward, to be honest, since I feel like OSX is providing the illusion that you have a functional python install, when really you just have permission to sometimes touch the system's python.
I just think this is the least evil approach, given the constraint that asking people to install a copy of the interpreter that they actually own is probably beyond a significant segment of the target audience.

I can give an example of the feature at work for the faulty numpydoc package mentioned in #1584.

The numpydoc package has a run-time requirement for sphinx. When conda-build is running, it will create an environment with the package being built (numpydoc) installed together with it's dependencies (which includes sphinx). This environment is used to perform import tests, which I guess only checks if the package can be imported without raising an exception.

I propose to add the following steps to the import testing step:

For each package in the environment:
* grab the version according to `conda list`
* grab the version according to `{package}.__version__` (if the attribute exists)
* if both versions could be collected:
  * assert that the versions match, otherwise packaging has apparently gone wrong

This would have caught the faulty numpydoc package before the upload step.

cobc -x -debug -free MAIN.cbl
cobc -debug -free UTIL.cbl

OK, I know storage is managed separately and I probably should have left it out. Our best approach there is to probably create a python-azure-storage package and manage that version according to that project.

I also suppose it is fair to call the current pypi "package" azure as it is a conglomeration of azure related Python modules, even if they are only used by reference and all these have their own version. Still this creates a big nightmare if help is needed by someone as it is way to easy to do a "pip install azure", which provides a certain version of the components but then do a "pip install A_COMPONENT" and lead the user right into potential trouble with compatibility or other issues.

Thus if the intention is to manage everything other than storage as one it would appear to be less risky for users to have a python-azure-storage release on pypi and have the azure release point to it + storage.

On the other hand if things go in the direction that different teams rather be on their own island than collaborate with each other it may be better to not have the top level azure-python-sdk and just have separately managed projects for the other components just like storage. 

So with storage set aside let me ask, if the intention is to keep azure-python-sdk together why not have a azure-python-sdk package on pypi that reflects the release state, and most important include the code, of this project? And if you are not willing to do that, can we at least get the release tarballs on GitHub signed, such that those downloading the tarball can at least verify that the downloaded tarball matches what those that released the code intended to be downloaded.

The code is written good and well tested, but let's discuss the usage design.
Now the API is:
```
session = aiohttp.ClientSession()
auth = aiohttp.DigestAuth('user', 'password', client)
resp = await auth.request('get', '/')
```
The API is not very handy:
1. `async with auth.request('get', url) as resp:` is not supported
2. `await auth.get(url)` is not supported
3 `async with client.get(url, auth=aiohttp.DigestAuth(...)` is not supported

We could fix 1. and 2. easy but it doesn't make a value.
What is really desirable is accepting `DigestAuth` as `auth` parameter -- like `requests` does [1]
Later we can add support for OAuth and other authorization protocols.
The approach requires inviting an abstract interface for auth classes and supporting it by both `BasicAuth` and `DigestAuth`.

[1]: http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/user/authentication/#digest-authentication

The problem is: if `charset` was not provided by `Content-Type` header applying `ISO-8859-1` might corrupt non-English text.
As I said now most part of non-English htmls are encoded by UTF-8 (at least should be). `ISO-8859-1` decodes it without an error but resulting text is obviously broken.
That's why I think the current behavior should not be changed.

But **documentation update** with mentioning possible performance issue and `resp.text('ISO-8859-1')` would be fine.

More more thoughts:
When composing multiple apps, cross-reference to URLs in other apps may happen in a specific direction only: from outer apps to inner apps, not to parent/sibling apps.

From the perspective of inner apps, it's safe to assume that the aiohttp's web framework will take care of filling outer prefix variables with the handler-local match_info context.

From the perspective of outer apps, they need to explicitly give the value of perfix variables when calling `url_for()` of routes of inner apps. In this specific case, we need a way to distinguish the duplicate variable names in the prefix names.

Hello @sbidoul
Yes, we can.

Good cases:
=========

```xml
<odoo noupdate="0">
</odoo>

<odoo noupdate="1">
</odoo>

<odoo>
</odoo>
```

Bad cases:
========
```xml
<odoo>
    <data noupdate="0">
    </data>
</odoo>

<odoo>
    <data noupdate="1">
    </data>
</odoo>

<odoo>
    <data>
    </data>
</odoo>
```

Examples from odoo:
  - [10.0 - addons/crm/data/crm_stage_data.xml#L2](https://github.com/odoo/odoo/blame/65e49baedeeab52ad9ba9c1e001449527118197a/addons/crm/data/crm_stage_data.xml#L2)
 - [10.0 - addons/payment/data/payment_acquirer_data.xml#L2](https://github.com/odoo/odoo/blame/65e49baedeeab52ad9ba9c1e001449527118197a/addons/payment/data/payment_acquirer_data.xml#L2)
 - [10.0 - addons/website_sale/data/demo.xml#L2](https://github.com/odoo/odoo/blame/65e49baedeeab52ad9ba9c1e001449527118197a/addons/website_sale/data/demo.xml#L2)

Use `<odoo noupdate="1">` node instead of `<odoo><data noupdate="1">`

Currently we have a convention to use 4 spaces for xml files, using the additional node `noupdate` force us to remove or add indentation.

Example:

Diffs using just `odoo` node (don't require add a new node and don't require re-ident):

```diff
-<odoo>
+<odoo noupdate="1">
    <record 1...
    <record 2...
    <record 3...
</odoo>
```

Diff using `odoo/data` node (re-ident is required if data wasn't defined to add `noupdate=1`):

```diff
<odoo>
+    <data noupdate="1">
-    <record 1...
+        <record 1...
-     <record 2...
+        <record 2...
-    <record 3...
+        <record 3...
+    </data>
</odoo>
```

Yes that is right too.

aggregated feeds are connected to the user feed.

perhaps you deleted them or you created a new app from the dashboard that
does not have these feeds.

I experimented with using project template Class Library (.Net Standard). When just created such a project references Microsoft.NETCore.platforms ( 1.1.0), which is confusing, but I think it's not wrong. It's easy to forget that .Netstandard is a specification, not a framework and .Netstandard supports .Net.Core 1.1. After you add the propertygroup below to the project directory, the project will however create both a .Net Core 2.0 and .Net 4.6.1 dll. Try it out yourself.

This is getting slightly debatable, so here's the arguments we've had over the IRC laid down.

I believe adding executing ```boulder-start.sh``` on boot would make it way easier since you'd only need to execute ```./tests/boulder-integration.sh```, instead of running ```./tests/boulder-start.sh```, waiting for it to finish, . That's the way I personally have it set up, makes it way easier.

However, as @bmw noted, that would conflict with the docs. Specifically, ```docs/contributing.rst``` which specify that ```boulder-start``` should be executed before ```boulder-integration```.

As ErikRose (without an @ before he asked not to be added to the thread), it would be nice to have Vagrant running as a box where the only thing required to execute the tests would be simply running them.

He also mentioned (and I quote) "Typically, my contribution docs say "Use the vagrant box. Alternately, if you really must, here's a long list of instructions to set it up on your own machine." - which I personally agree with.

Therefore, I'd think it'd be really nice to add running ```./tests/boulder-start.sh``` on boot, and modifying the docs to reflect that.

I have a function called `poll_many()` which does exactly that.

Suppose it's collecting messages, it has called poll() five times and got five messages, and while it's polling a sixth time there's an error. What is it going to do?

If it raises an exception, there's the danger that these five messages are going to be committed although they have never been used. (We use autocommit=False, however I'm not certain what `poll_many()`'s caller is going to do.) Is it possible to tell confluent_kafka/librdkafka "pretend you never read these messages", i.e. put them back to the incoming buffer, or reset the offset, but without committing anything?

I have a model that encompasses a large hierarchy (set up as a self-join with the standard parent/children FKs).  Then, I've set up different serializers to reflect different subsets of that main model (based on a `level` field.).  Each individual serializer works fine except that the type of the model returned by the `relationships` field reflects the overarching model as opposed to the `resource_name` specified (either in view or serializer).


To explain more fully:

Assume a single model called "Entity", with the following basic structure:

```
name = CharField()
kind = CharField(choices...)
parent = models.ForeignKey(
    'self',
    null=True,
    related_name='children',
)
```

For the `kind` field, assume it follows a geographical hierarchy such as "Country", "State", "City".  Thus, an `entity` instance could be named "USA", and have children named "California", "Nevada", etc,, and then the `entity` instance named "California" would have a parent of "USA" and children including "San Francisco", "Los Angeles", etc.   

For my purposes, I'm trying to expose endpoints that correspond to the various kinds `api/countries`, `api/states` etc., and have set up the filtered ModelViewSets and Serializers accordingly, with the specified `resource_name` on each serializer to reflect the proper type. 

The renderer will return the instance with the correct resource name as specified, if requesting through `api/state` then the instance type will be "state".  However, the relationship instances' types are called by the master model (in my example, "Entity").  This is causing problems for my clients, since they don't know what the Entity is -- I'm hoping to have that simply be an internal reference.  

I've tried to dig into the code to see how the type is set, but it is referred to in so many places that I"m getting lost. So my questions are:

- Is it reasonable that you could refer to different parent/child types in this way?  
- What is the proper way to ensure the type is specified to my needs, particularly since the parent/child relationship types rely more on the "Kind" of the individual instance rather than the instance type itself.

@martin-neuhaeusser @wintersteiger I've been reading this discussion with interest.

I have not needed to use Z3's ``mk_to_ieee_bv`` yet but I suspect I will soon and this issue with NaNs looks a little worrying.

I understand that because there are multiple bit representations of NaN there cannot be a SMT-LIB function ``mk_to_ieee_bv`` as a function cannot map one value (i.e. NaN) to multiple different values.

I have a suggestion for handling this in Z3 and in SMT-LIB. A function ``fp.to_ieee_bv`` could be added that takes an additional argument which is the value that should be returned if the floating point argument is a NaN.

e.g.

```
(declare-fun x() Float32)
(assert (fp.isNaN x))
(assert (= (fp.to_ieee_bv x #x7fc00000) #x7fc00000))
(check-sat)
sat
```

```
(declare-fun x() Float32)
(assert (fp.isNaN x))
(assert (distinct (= (fp.to_ieee_bv x #x7fc00000) #x7fc00000)))
(check-sat)
unsat
```

and another way of representing NaN

```
(declare-fun x() Float32)
(assert (fp.isNaN x))
(assert (= (fp.to_ieee_bv x #x7f800001) #x7f800001))
(check-sat)
sat
```

To me this seems like the right solution as it gives the user control of what to return if the input is a NaN rather than forcing the implementer of an SMT solver to make an arbitrary choice on which bit pattern should be returned when given a NaN.

> Another use case that comes to my mind is to extract the last time step hidden units of a ReccurrentLayer with sequence length (e.g. the last 1 in the mask). Currently, I did not find a way of doing it as well, as it depends on the mask, thus I produce a second mask which is [0,0...,1,.0,0], where 1 indicates the last time step.

You could instead change your data to left-pad the sequences:
```
inputs = [[0, 0, 0, something, something],
          [0, 0, something, something, something]]
masks = [[0, 0, 0, 1, 1],
         [0, 0, 1, 1, 1]]
```
Then you can still just slice out the last element.

In principle, it shouldn't be problematic to extend `SliceLayer` to support symbolic values, though. `get_output_shape_for` would need to be aware of that and return `None` for the sliced dimension, but that's all. Feel free to send a PR!

For clarity, there are three different ways to copy in Ganga:

1. `import copy; copy.copy(obj)` this copies the object but keeps all references intact below that. It is overridden by implementing `__copy__` in your class.
2. `import copy; copy.deepcopy(obj)` this clones every layer recursively.  It is overridden by implementing `__deepcopy__` in your class
3. `j = Job(); j.copy()` this is a Ganga-specific thing which exists only in the proxy layer which calls `GangaObject.clone()`. Really it neither copies, nor clones but rather "makes another object which is similar but with some differences" such as unpreparing etc. All three are doing very different things at various points.

In general I'm now starting to see problems with the session locks. The problem with them is that they are done using flock (or lockf) which lock files per process. This means that two threads in the same process are not protected by the session lock. I'm seeing situations where multiple threads are trying to interact with the session lock simultaneously which is causing some strange interference.

I guess the first way to fix this would be to put a thread lock on the `SessionLock` class which technically would be a solution. However, this is starting to get very complicated and it's not clear to me what the canonical source of information about an object is, what are read-only views and what are free, lockless objects. Currently a `GangaObject` can be in multiple processes, multiple registries, multiple threads and maybe none of the above.

def test(n_cores=4, flist=flist):

    pt.pmap(n_cores, pt.radgyr, traj)

mdtraj code
```python
import pytraj as pt
from glob import glob
from pytraj.testing import Timer
import mdtraj as md

flist = sorted(glob('./remd.x.0*'))[:30]
top = md.load_topology('./myparm.parm7')

@Timer()
def test(flist=flist):
    for fname in flist:
        print(fname)
        traj = md.load_netcdf(fname, top=top)
        md.compute_rg(traj)

test()
```

This seems to be working for me. Code inspired by many sources.

```
from tornado.web import HTTPError
from tornado.web import RequestHandler
from opentracing_instrumentation import http_server
from opentracing_instrumentation import request_context


class BaseRequestHandler(RequestHandler):
    def _execute(self, *args, **kwargs):
        request = http_server.TornadoRequestWrapper(self.request)
        self.span = http_server.before_request(request)

        with request_context.span_in_stack_context(self.span):
            super(BaseRequestHandler, self)._execute(*args, **kwargs)

    def log_exception(self, typ, value, tb):
        if not isinstance(value, HTTPError) or 500 <= value.status_code <= 599:
            self.span.set_tag('error', True)
            self.span.log_event(event='error', payload=value)

        super(BaseRequestHandler, self).log_exception(typ, value, tb)

    def on_finish(self):
        self.span.set_tag('http.status_code', self.get_status())
        self.span.finish()
```

You need more than just HDLC or FLEN for a dedicated sniffer app. You need some way to set the channel, etc. If this capability is going to be built into the NCP (which I plan to do), might as well use that.

For example,  you can turn any spinel-based NCP into a sniffer (not considering the framing protocol) by sending it the following commands:

* `CMD_RESET`
    * Bytes: `80 01`
* `CMD_SET_PROP(PROP_PHY_ENABLED, 1)`
    * Bytes: `80 03 20 01`
* `CMD_SET_PROP(PROP_PHY_RAW_STREAM_ENABLED, 1)`
    * Bytes: `80 03 27 01`
* `CMD_SET_PROP(PROP_PHY_PROMISCUOUS_MODE_ENABLED, 1)`
    * Bytes: `80 03 28 01`
* `CMD_SET_PROP(PROP_PHY_CHAN, <Channel-Number>)`
    * Bytes: `80 03 21 XX` (where `XX` is the channel number in hex)

Then you would get raw frames on the given channel via updates to `PROP_STREAM_RAW`. The frames from the NCP would look like this:

    80 06 71 LL LL RR ... RR MM ... MM

Where:

* `LL LL` is the length of the raw packet (`XX ... XX`) in bytes, stored as a little-endian uint16.
* `RR ... RR` is the bytes of the raw packet.
* `MM ... MM` is optional metadata about the raw packet (Channel, RSSI, LQI, etc). Format TBD.

So, all things considered, it's really not any more complicated to talk to the NCP than it would be to talk to a dedicated sniffer app. Initialization is just sending the NCP a fixed set of frames, setting the last byte of the last frame to the channel. Then you handle any frame you receive starting with `80 06 71` as a received packet, and extract the packet bytes and metadata.

Note that this isn't actually implemented in the NCP yet, but doing so wouldn't be very difficult and is absolutely on my list of capabilities I intend to add.

I took another stab at this in light of @dean0x7d 's comments and some other issues to simplify the interface and implementation.  Most of the issues related to alias classes.  The implementation is a bit lighter, but the code logic grew a bit to handle the special cases aliases bring up.

Summary of changes:
- renamed `py::init_factory` to `py::init`.  This is easy to revert, but this doesn't conflict with the templated no-argument `py::init<...>()`, while keeping the naming nicer (especially if we decide to roll this into the core, documented approach replacing raw placement-new `__init__`s).
- factories can no longer return types *convertible* to the base/alias class: they can now return only a {base,alias} {value,pointer}, a holder, or a shared_ptr<T> to some base or derived `T` (only applies when the class has a `shared_ptr` holder to begin with).
- cutting out the convertible support allowed the implementation to be a bit lighter by avoiding the need for the tag classes (which were needed to prioritize ambiguous calls).
- If returning a base class (whether by value or pointer) when an alias is required (i.e. python-side inheritance and a declared alias), a dynamic_cast to the alias is attempted (for the pointer version); if it fails, or if returned by value, an Alias(Class &&) constructor needs to be defined (otherwise a type_error is thrown).
- for holder returns when an alias is required, we try a dynamic_cast of the pointer to the alias; if it fails, we raise an error.
- a new `py::init(class_factory, alias_factory)` function is available that takes two factories: the first is called when an alias is not needed, the second when it is.  (This, in particular, is how holder-returning factories can still be used, but puts the onus on the caller to sort out the base/alias creation logic).

(I'm holding off on the writing the documentation until the implementation looks good).

To better explain what we need to do here, we want to:

1. Hide `--renew-hook`. This can be done by setting `help=argparse.SUPPRESS` when the `--renew-hook` command is added in `cli.py`.
2. Add a `--deploy-hook` flag that works the same as `--renew-hook` except it is also called after the cert is obtained for the first time.

How exactly this is done can be left up to the implementator, but the way I'd probably do it is to set `dest='renew_hook'` when adding `--deploy-hook` to `cli.py`. This preserves forward compatibility so when people obtain a cert providing `--deploy-hook` the hook will still be run during renewal with older versions of Certbot. (Downgrading your Certbot version happens from time to time as people switch from `certbot-auto` to OS packages.)

The only other piece I believe you need is a way to differentiate whether `renew_hook` in a config was set by `--deploy-hook` or `--renew-hook` so if you're obtaining a cert for the first time you only run the hook if it was set by `--deploy-hook`. The way I'd do this is by using `certbot.cli.set_by_cli`.
