I think the point of bundling should be bundling of input and output files, not necessarily the intermediate files, and the primary purpose should be for reproducible analysis. That is to say, if we bundle a project, the project should be able to reproduce by itself, except for certain verifiable requirements (executables, remote hosts). That is to say, if we can include the following in bundles

1. meta data (keywords, descriptions etc)
2. "external" requirements (executable targets, reference genome files, etc)
3. input and output data

The bundled package should be reproducible as long as the external requirements are met.

If we leave "external" requirements outside of bundle, we can create tar files with required files, and allow the reproducibility of bundles when external requirements are met. If we would like to keep "external" requirements, we should create docker images for the bundles so that the workflow can be executed. I am not sure how to achieve the latter though.

When Ray starts up, it prints out how much memory it reserves for the object store, e.g.,

```
Allowing the Plasma store to use up to 13.7439GB of memory.
```

On Linux this should be about 40% of the total memory. What is this number in your case? And how much memory do you expect your application to need?

Normally when the object store fills up, it tries to evict some objects. You can see this behavior by running a loop like

```python
import numpy as np
import ray

ray.init()

x = np.random.normal(size=1000000)

while True:
    ray.put(x)
```

Eventually it will print something like

```
There is not enough space to create this object, so evicting 344 objects to free up 2752305472 bytes.
```

If it isn't evicting stuff in your case, that could mean one of the following:

1) You're trying to create a single object that is so large it can't fit in the object store.
2) Many of the objects in the object store are still in use by some worker so they can't be evicted. This should only happen if numpy arrays are involved.

As far as I can tell `python-librtmp` is only used in the ustream plugin. It is used to talk to rtmp servers, doing rpc, etc. which is generally not required rtmpdump is usually sufficient. I don't see any point trying to support it at the moment. 

Ustream desktop streams are either fragmented flv or fragmented mp4, which are very similar, both are h264 and aac I think. It seems like their source of truth for each stream is the mp4/flv, and if you want to watch it on mobile (iOS) then you need an HLS stream which needs to be mpeg ts (iOS 10, supports mp4 in HLS). They don't appear to generate the HLS streams, m3u8 and the mepg ts fragments, unless one is requested and it can take a while for the HLS stream to appear. I'd imagine they lag behind the desktop streams as well, but I haven't tested that to confirm. The video content is likely exactly exactly the same, just in a different container format. Although I think the desktop streams have 720p in higher bit rate. 
It's true that the HLS stream handling code is already implemented, which is good and we can continue to expose the mobile streams for ustream.

The fragmented mp4 streams have separate video and audio streams and need to be muxed. They are created to the MPEG DASH spec, adding support for the fragmented mp4 streams (the flv streams are already supported) will be good for the project as a whole. It paves the way for MPEG DASH support, the next thing on my list...

The key is a bit of encrypted text that enables you to play the stream. Your browser (or maybe more accurately, the video player) sees the key, and then uses it to decrypt the individual video segments that it reads from the m3u8 playlist. To open the stream with streamlink, streamlink needs the key, or else it will just be stuck with a bunch of video segments that it can't do anything with.

Since the key is usually listed in the m3u8 file, streamlink already knows where it is. It just can't download it because it is forbidden/unauthorized to open with just the URL. You need some kind of authorization token to prove that you are a user that can access the stream. For FOX Sports Go streams, you would need two cookies—`access_token` and `authorization`. For ESPN+ streams, its in the form of an `authorization` header that gets passed from your browser. Headers are how websites know, for example, what browser and/or device you're using, so that it can display the website appropriately. In this case, it's how the website knows that you are a logged in/subscribed user.

Now, you need to pass that header into streamlink, which means you need to know what it is. Google Chrome has a great "URL snooper," found in the Network tab of Developer Tools. I'm not sure for other browsers, but I'm sure you could find an add-on that would do the same. Reload the page, then once the stream appears, search for `key`. For some reason, I usually get two entries, but if you click on one, and scroll down, you'll see a _long_ string of what looks like random text. This is what you want. Copy it. Then, when you go back to streamlink. Your command should be `streamlink --http-header "authorization=*************************" "hlsvariant://https://aeng.svcs.plus.espn.com/settoken/redirect/v1?sect=espnplus:web:vod:en&ssess=2c7d41be-f5b8-48aa-8fc7-e4c888f6cf0d&trk=s&platform=web&app=espn&partnr=espn&uid={4FF60D4B-D364-4BC4-B60D-4BD3643BC4D5}&swid={4FF60D4B-D364-4BC4-B60D-4BD3643BC4D5}&cdn=AKAMAI-SECURE&corigin=CLT2&mediaurl=https://hlslive-akc-clt2.media.plus.espn.com/hdnts=exp=1531496041~acl=/*~id=804f39bc-946e-4546-b344-d365244bdac7~data=883f1ddb-4975-4319-81b4-c063ae3c131f~hmac=4d93596fc8da3fc86434e3bdefbb9e20277e259441b0feecd381892ca3b3eaa6/a20ee2a6ba3e4f73cdf5c3c352428a55/ls01/espn/event/2018/07/12/2018_FIL_World_Lacrosse_C_20180712_1531364848960/master_desktop_complete_aeng-trimmed.m3u8" best`, along with whatever other parameters you want passed in. Note that the authorization header will be much longer than what I put there. This enables streamlink, once it sees that it needs a key, to use that header to pretend like it is viewing the stream in the browser in order to get the key, and then it can decrypt the stream and play it/save it.

@ncoghlan: Interesting point! The requirement to detect *unawaited* coroutines makes this a bit complicated; a list of every live coroutine is less useful, because we can't afford to iterate over the list checking if each one is unawaited after each scheduling step -- that would be effectively quadratic. A possible refinement would be to track a list of all coroutines + a counter of unawaited coroutines, so we use the counter to detect the problem and then scan the list to diagnose it. There still are some possible issues with coroutines that get garbage collected before we notice the problem, and with cases where there are independent coroutine schedulers running in different threads... I'd have to think about that more.

I'm using this hack:

    class JSRaw(int):
        """Hack to allow including raw Javascript code in simplejson.dumps
        output. Objects of this sort are dumped as raw strings.
        """
        def __new__(self, string):
            instance = int.__new__(self, 0)
            instance.string= string
            return instance
        def __repr__(self):
            return self.string
        def __unicode__(self):
            return self.string
        def __str__(self):
            return self.string

Example:

    dumps({'function': JSRaw('function() { console.write(foo); }')})
